title: George Grass Jr. (1819-1887): A Farmer of Wakefield, Carleton County, on the Upper Saint John River
narrative: George Grass Jr. was born 8 June 1819 in New Brunswick, the son of George Grass (Sr.) and Eunice (Bernice) Kinney. The family belonged to the cluster of Grass / DeGrass / DeGrasse households of the upper Saint John River valley in what became Carleton and neighboring Sunbury counties. His mother's family, the Kinneys, is associated with Oromocto in Sunbury County and, by the second quarter of the nineteenth century, with the Carleton County backcountry, fitting the broad pattern by which families moved up the Saint John River into the newly surveyed parishes above Woodstock. George's exact birthplace within New Brunswick is not pinned down in the free records consulted; community family trees most often place his life in Wakefield Parish, the district where he later married, farmed, and died.

The surname itself is unstable in the record, appearing as Grass, DeGrass, and DeGrasse across different documents and trees - a normal feature of nineteenth-century clerical spelling rather than evidence of any single origin. The 'De' form is sometimes taken to hint at a Continental root, whether a German 'Grass' or a French 'DeGrasse,' and to suggest a Loyalist or disbanded-soldier ancestry of the kind that founded so much of Carleton County. That is offered here only as an unproven possibility. It should not be confused with the well-known Loyalist Captain Michael Grass, who led a party of Associated Loyalists to Cataraqui (modern Kingston, Ontario) rather than to New Brunswick; no documented link between that family and George's line appears in the free sources, and none is asserted here.

The question of George's wife is the most tangled part of his record, and the surviving sources actually point to two successive marriages rather than to conflicting names for one woman. Community genealogies record a first wife, Susanna (given in some trees as a Drake), who is said to have died 14 May 1855 in Wakefield, Carleton County; and a second marriage later that same year, with a register entry to the effect that 'George Grass of the Parish of Wakefield and Nancy [Anne] Everett of the same place' were married on 22 September 1855 by Thomas Todd, a Baptist minister. This sequence would neatly explain why the names Annie, Nancy, and Susanna all attach to 'Mrs. Grass' in the record. Because the supporting detail comes largely from secondary and community-compiled trees rather than from a register page examined here, the two-wife reading is presented as the most likely reconciliation of the evidence, not as a settled fact, and no single given name is asserted as 'the' wife.

George Grass Jr. lived the life of a farmer in the Carleton County borderland, in country that the British government had opened to settlement only a generation or two before his birth. Wakefield Parish had been erected in 1803 on the west bank of the Saint John above Woodstock, and Carleton County itself was set off from York County in 1831, the year George turned twelve. The region was first peopled by disbanded soldiers of the Revolution and of the War of 1812, who took up land grants laid out in the military surveys of 1817 along the river, and then by Scottish and Irish immigrants in the following decades. The 'interval' land along the Saint John was prized for farming - hay, oats, potatoes, and stock - while the surrounding forest fed an important timber trade. This is the documented setting of George's working life; the free sources do not detail his particular farm, acreage, or crops, so the texture of his days is drawn from the history of his parish and county rather than from records naming him at the plough.

His was emphatically a border life. Wakefield Parish had once stretched into territory that New Brunswick and the United States both claimed, and it was only the Webster-Ashburton Treaty of 1842 that fixed the international line; the county's western edge runs against Aroostook County, Maine. Families on the upper Saint John moved, married, and traded back and forth across that border throughout the nineteenth century, and it is exactly this New Brunswick-to-Maine borderland that connects George's household to the wider family tree these notes belong to. The named children associated with George in the community trees - among them Henrietta Sophia, James Alfred, Jonathan Woodford, Lory M., Flora Annett, and Elizabeth Blanch Grass, with other trees adding such names as Densmore, Norman, and a daughter who married into the Lenentine family - represent the generation through which the line carries forward, though the full and exact roster of his children, and which were born to which wife, is not established here with confidence.

George Grass Jr. died 2 March 1887 at Wakefield, Carleton County, New Brunswick, in the same parish that had framed most of his adult life, at the age of sixty-seven. He had been born when the surrounding county did not yet exist as such and the Maine boundary was still in dispute, and he died in a settled, named, mapped Carleton County of farms and river towns - his lifespan tracing almost exactly the consolidation of the upper Saint John valley from a contested military frontier into established New Brunswick farming country.

What can be stated with confidence about George Grass Jr. is solid if modest: a man born 8 June 1819 in New Brunswick to George Grass Sr. and Eunice Kinney; a farmer of Wakefield Parish in Carleton County, in the upper Saint John River borderland with Maine; married - most probably twice, the names Susanna, Nancy, and Anne Everett belonging in that picture - and the father of a family that carries the line forward; and dead at Wakefield on 2 March 1887. The variant surname (Grass / DeGrass / DeGrasse) and any Loyalist or Continental origin behind it remain open questions, flagged here as possibilities rather than facts, in keeping with what the free documentary record can honestly support.
